label: CAMPAIGN 05
title: The Language of the Economy
firewall: We track how the words have been used and how fast they are winning, not whether the underlying politics is correct.
summary: One economy, three names. Inflation hit a three-year high while markets set records, and the same economy acquired three incompatible vocabularies: the institutional one that measures a rate, the populist one that feels a price level, and the political one that assigns blame.
anchor: June 2026: consumer inflation hit a three-year high on an energy and tariff shock. Equity markets set records, yet roughly two of three households reported cutting back, with a Fed leadership handoff and the 2026 midterms underneath. The data and the experience point in opposite directions. Three vocabularies have formed to name the gap.
thesis: One economy, three names. Inflation hit a three-year high while markets set records, and the same economy acquired three incompatible vocabularies: the institutional one that measures a rate, the populist one that feels a price level, and the political one that assigns blame.

Contextive tracks how fast each name is winning, measured against open coverage data rather than estimated. The institutional optimism vocabulary is fading exactly as the populist vocabulary surges. The defining tension: 'soft landing' versus 'vibecession.'

definingTension.note: The cycle's defining collision, and it is measured. As inflation re-accelerated, the institutional optimism term faded while the populist pessimism term surged. The same data turning produced opposite vocabulary movements. The vibe word is winning the argument the data word is presumed to be winning.

clusters.name: Populist / lived
clusters.blurb: How the economy is named from the checkout line, where the feeling is the fact: a price level, not a rate. It moves fastest when official data improves while prices stay high, because that gap is the vocabulary's whole territory.

clusters.name: Institutional / macro
clusters.blurb: The vocabulary of the Fed, the analyst class, and the markets desk, engineered for precision: a rate, a target, a path. Technically correct, rhetorically losing when the rate and the level diverge.

clusters.name: Political-blame
clusters.blurb: The vocabulary that converts the economy into a verdict: attribution devices that assign a cause and a culprit. Symmetric, since every term has been deployed by whichever coalition is out of power.

pillars.title: The vocabulary of an economy that doesn't feel like the data
pillars.body: Cluster A against the inflation print (vibecession, affordability, shrinkflation, greedflation), with the data-versus-vibes gap as the spine. The highest-recognition piece: every reader has paid the prices.

pillars.title: One number, three vocabularies
pillars.body: A single inflation print as the unit of analysis, tracking how the rate vocabulary, the level vocabulary, and the blame vocabulary name the same figure. The cycle's most analytical argument: a rate and a level are different objects.

pillars.title: The signal issue
pillars.body: What the inflation spike looks like through the velocity index: the highest-velocity terms against the declining ones, showing the signal layer working in real time on the live print.

tensions.title: Soft landing vs vibecession
tensions.body: The cycle's defining collision, and it is measured. As inflation re-accelerated, the institutional optimism term faded while the populist pessimism term surged. The same data turning produced opposite vocabulary movements. The vibe word is winning the argument the data word is presumed to be winning.

tensions.title: Disinflation vs affordability
tensions.body: Rate versus level. 'Disinflation' is the precise term for prices rising more slowly; 'affordability' is the populist term for prices that are still high. Both describe the same economy truthfully, with opposite emotional valence. The institutional metric can improve every month while the populist metric never falls.

tensions.title: Greedflation vs [admin]flation
tensions.body: Two attribution vocabularies competing to assign the cause of high prices: the firms or the government. Measured, 'greedflation' is in decline, a burned out frame, while partisan attribution naming intensifies into the midterms. The blame is migrating from the corporation to the incumbent.

tensions.title: K-shaped vs real wages
tensions.body: The institutional bridge term. 'K-shaped' is analyst vocabulary that concedes the populist point: aggregate 'real wages' can rise while the median experience splits between asset holders and wage earners. It is the rare institutional term that validates the populist complaint in the institutional cluster's own language.

tensions.title: Tariff tax vs soft landing
tensions.body: Is the policy a cost or a strategy. 'Tariff tax' reframes tariffs as a consumer tax paid at home, and the energy and tariff shock is precisely what ended the 'soft landing' narrative this cycle. The collision is over who pays, and whether naming the cost is itself a partisan act.

corpusDensity.note: GDELT coverage-intensity proxy (share of monitored coverage), not literal mentions/M

terms.whatItDoes: Names the gap between strong macro data and negative public sentiment, and resolves it in favor of the sentiment. The feeling is the fact; the 'real economy' is the one experienced at the register, not the one reported in aggregate. It turns a measurement dispute into a lived-truth claim no chart can directly rebut.
terms.whereContested: Economists argue sentiment lags the data, is distorted by partisanship and negative-news selection, and mistakes mood for material conditions. Users counter that the costs behind the mood (rent, groceries, insurance) are real and not captured by a falling inflation rate. As the re-acceleration arrived, the contest shifted from 'sentiment is irrational' to 'sentiment was early.'

verbatims.quote: Wall Street keeps rising, even as U.S. households keep getting more discouraged.

corpusDensity.note: GDELT coverage-intensity proxy (share of monitored coverage), not literal mentions/M

terms.whatItDoes: Attributes price increases to corporate profit-seeking rather than to monetary, supply, or energy factors, relocating blame from policy to firms. It is an attribution device in economic costume: 'flation' borrows the authority of the inflation discourse while 'greed' supplies the verdict.
terms.whereContested: Some analyses found margin expansion in the 2021–23 window; others hold it explains a small, self-reversing share of inflation. Measured, it is the cycle's clearest decliner, a burned out frame being displaced by government-attribution vocabulary as the midterms approach. The contest is no longer about accuracy but about whether the term has been outcompeted.

terms.whatItDoes: Names product downsizing at a constant price as hidden inflation, making an abstraction physically visible: the cereal box you can hold and compare. Its power is evidentiary. It turns a statistic into a photograph, which is why it travels on visual platforms faster than any other term in the cluster.
terms.whereContested: Firms frame it as cost management and reformulation; consumer advocates frame it as deception. It is genuinely measurable and genuinely small as a share of headline inflation, so the contest is over salience. Critics say it distracts from larger drivers; users say it is the most legible proof that the official number understates lived cost.

corpusDensity.note: GDELT coverage-intensity proxy (share of monitored coverage), not literal mentions/M

terms.whatItDoes: Reframes add-on charges as illegitimate by construction: 'junk' is the verdict the term carries before any specific fee is examined. It is a regulatory-target frame, where naming a category 'junk fees' is the first step toward acting on it.
terms.whereContested: Industry argues the fees reflect real, optional, or unbundled costs and that mandated all-in pricing reduces choice; advocates argue the fees exist to obscure the true price until the buyer is committed. The contest is over whether the practice is information or deception, and the word has already decided.

corpusDensity.note: GDELT coverage-intensity proxy (share of monitored coverage), not literal mentions/M

terms.whatItDoes: Names a price increase as exploitation by invoking a moral and, in many states, legal category. It presupposes a baseline 'fair' price against which the current price is a violation, converting an economic event into a wrong with a wrongdoer.
terms.whereContested: Most economists warn that anti-gouging rules suppress the price signal that resolves shortages; advocates and many voters treat the fairness claim as primary, especially on essentials. The term sits on a genuine disagreement about whether prices are information or moral facts, and it is rising as a political frame regardless of where the economics lands.

corpusDensity.note: GDELT coverage-intensity proxy (share of monitored coverage), not literal mentions/M

terms.whatItDoes: Shifts the frame from the inflation rate (a derivative) to the price level (a stock). 'Affordability' is true whether inflation is rising or falling: prices can stop climbing and still be unaffordable. That makes it the most durable populist term in the cluster, and the hardest for the institutional vocabulary to answer in its own metrics.
terms.whereContested: The institutional cluster struggles here because its central success metric (a falling rate) does not move the populist metric (the level). Critics say 'affordability' is too elastic to measure; users say its imprecision is exactly why it captures the experience the indices miss.

verbatims.quote: Tomato prices are up 40%, the latest symbol of America's affordability squeeze.

corpusDensity.note: GDELT coverage-intensity proxy (share of monitored coverage), not literal mentions/M

terms.whatItDoes: Names the scenario in which inflation cools without a recession, and serves as a narrative anchor for markets and the central bank: a target the institutional cluster organizes optimism around. It is a path described as if it were a destination.
terms.whereContested: It is a path, not a promise, and one hot print flips it. Measured, the renewed inflation is dissolving it while the successor frame 'no landing' rises. The term is moving from the cluster's confident default to a claim it now has to defend, which is why it is classified contested rather than saturating.

verbatims.quote: Key inflation gauge hits multiyear high as American consumer confidence slides.

terms.whatItDoes: Names a slowing in the rate of price increases, precisely and technically, in a way the public systematically mishears as 'prices are coming down' (they are not). The term's precision is its communication failure: disinflation is not deflation, and the distance between the two is where the populist cluster lives.
terms.whereContested: Not contested on the merits, since it is the correct word, but contested in effect. The institutional cluster keeps reporting disinflation as good news to an audience that experiences a price level, not a derivative. The term is technically unimpeachable and rhetorically self-defeating, the purest example of the cluster's structural problem.

corpusDensity.note: GDELT coverage-intensity proxy (share of monitored coverage), not literal mentions/M

terms.whatItDoes: Sets an expectation: policy rates stay elevated longer than markets hope. It is a forward-guidance phrase that does work by managing anticipation, since naming the stance is part of executing it.
terms.whereContested: Read as prudence by hawks and as overcorrection by doves. With the re-acceleration the phrase is back from near-retirement. The contest is over whether sustained restriction is discipline or damage, and the incoming central-bank posture is the term's next inflection.

terms.whatItDoes: Names a recovery that splits in two, asset holders rising and wage earners falling, in a single letter. It is the institutional cluster's most important term because it concedes, in analyst language, the populist cluster's central claim: that 'the economy' is two economies and the aggregate hides the split.
terms.whereContested: Some economists find the bifurcation overstated or cyclical; others treat it as the defining feature. Its significance is structural. It is the bridge term, the place where the institutional and populist vocabularies briefly agree.

verbatims.quote: As US stock market hits new highs, 2 of 3 Americans are cutting back on spending.

corpusDensity.note: GDELT coverage-intensity proxy (share of monitored coverage), not literal mentions/M

terms.whatItDoes: Names wages adjusted for inflation, the institutional cluster's direct answer to 'are people worse off.' It is the number wheeled out to rebut the vibecession claim, and on aggregate it often supports the rebuttal.
terms.whereContested: Aggregate real wages can rise while medians stall and essential-good prices outpace the basket; composition effects flatter the average. The term is accurate and incomplete, and the gap between the accurate average and the incomplete experience is where the argument never resolves.

terms.whatItDoes: Frames the stretch from roughly three percent down to the two percent target as the hardest part, a reassuring metaphor that casts persistence as expected rather than alarming. It manages expectations by naming the difficulty in advance.
terms.whereContested: With the re-acceleration, the 'last mile' framing reads as complacent in hindsight. Critics argue it minimized the risk that the mile runs backward. The contest is over whether the metaphor described a difficulty or excused a failure.

terms.whatItDoes: Reframes a tariff as a tax paid by domestic buyers rather than a tool against foreign producers, an attribution device that relocates the cost from 'them' to 'us.' Naming a tariff a tax is the whole argument, compressed.
terms.whereContested: Proponents argue tariffs protect strategic industry and supply leverage, and that pass-through is partial; most economists and the opposition argue importers and consumers bear the cost. The brand tracks both registers without adjudicating: the energy and tariff shock is the measured driver ending the soft-landing narrative, and naming that cost is itself contested as a partisan act.

verbatims.quote: Brass bands in Beijing make way for sticker shock at home.

terms.whatItDoes: Pins inflation to the sitting administration by welding its name to the condition, a portable attribution device. The construction is symmetric and reusable: it has been built against whichever party holds power, which is the tell that it is a blame tool, not an analysis.
terms.whereContested: Economists note that an administration has limited short-run control over a price level driven by the central bank, supply chains, and energy shocks; partisans deploy the term precisely because that causal modesty is hard to fit on a sign. The brand names the attribution pattern and its symmetry (every coalition uses it from opposition) and never ratifies the culprit.

verbatims.quote: Tax cuts collide with inflation as voters weigh the economy in the midterms.

terms.whatItDoes: Elevates the affordability frame to 'crisis,' importing urgency, a victim, and an implied responsible party. 'Crisis' converts a chronic condition into an emergency that demands a response and rewards whoever promises one.
terms.whereContested: Whether a 3.8% rate constitutes a 'crisis' is itself contested. Critics call the word inflationary in its own right; defenders point to the price level rather than the rate. It is used across the spectrum, which makes it a slogan more than a diagnosis, and the brand tracks the slogan's velocity, not its truth value.

terms.whatItDoes: Frames economic strain as systemic and terminal rather than cyclical, naming the system, not the cycle, as the cause. It absorbs a wide range of grievances (prices, housing, wages) into a single structural verdict, which is why it functions as a meme as much as an argument.
terms.whereContested: Critics call it fatalistic and analytically empty ('late' relative to what); users say it captures the persistence that cyclical vocabulary keeps failing to explain away. An adjacent 'oligarchy' framing is rising with billionaire-wealth discourse. The brand tracks the migration of cyclical complaint into structural vocabulary as a leading indicator.

corpusDensity.note: GDELT coverage-intensity proxy (share of monitored coverage), not literal mentions/M

terms.whatItDoes: Reactivates a classic campaign question to convert the economy into a retrospective referendum on the incumbent, a device that turns a complex aggregate into a yes/no a voter answers from their own checkbook.
terms.whereContested: It invites baseline-shopping: every side picks the comparison year that flatters its case, and the same data answers both ways. It is midterm-coded and symmetric, since the out-party always asks it. The brand tracks its cyclical reactivation, not the answer.
